summary:
The La Tène culture (c. 450–50 BC) was the developed Iron Age Celtic archaeological culture that succeeded Hallstatt and represents the historic Celts who spread across much of Europe. Named for a site on Lake Neuchâtel in Switzerland (where large quantities of votive metalwork were recovered from the lake), the La Tène culture is distinguished by a recognizable artistic style featuring swirling curvilinear designs, flowing plant motifs, and zoomorphic imagery applied to bronze and iron weapons, jewelry, and chariot fittings with extraordinary technical skill.

La Tène Celts were mobile and expansive: from their core in central Europe they spread into Gaul, Iberia, Britain, Ireland, the Balkans, and Anatolia (as Galatians) between the 5th and 3rd centuries BC. Their migrations included the [[event:1235|Gallic Invasion of Italy]] sack of Rome (390 BC) and the [[event:1205|Galatian Invasion of Greece]] (279 BC). Celtic languages spread with this expansion and survive today as Welsh, Irish, Scottish Gaelic, Breton, Cornish, and Manx. La Tène artistic style, characterized by its complex interlaced designs, profoundly influenced medieval Irish and Northumbrian art — including the Book of Kells — and is commonly recognized as a defining element of 'Celtic' visual identity.
